angle: What it's like building a product while working full-time
threads: Building kakisewa while working full-time is basically: 8am-6pm doing my actual job, then 9pm-midnight arguing with myself about database schemas. Currently cold emailing 8,471 Malaysian rental agents at 100/day. Did I have time to build this? No. Did I do it anyway? Unfortunately yes.

slides: What building a product while working full-time actually looks like
Your 'deep work' hours are 10pm on a Tuesday
You ship features in 90-minute windows between meetings
Every weekend is a negotiation between rest and roadmap
Currently cold emailing 8,471 rental agents. 100 a day. One by one.
It's not romantic. It's just stubbornness with a Notion doc.
But it's the only way I know how to find out if this is real
carousel.caption: kakisewa is getting built in the gaps. Sharing the whole thing as it happens.

angle: Day 1 — Building in public: zero paying customers
caption:
I built a SaaS and have zero paying customers.

Here is what week 1 actually looked like.

What are you building right now? Drop it in the comments.
threads_text:
I built a SaaS and have zero paying customers.

Here is what week 1 actually looked like: the launch nobody saw, the Stripe dashboard I kept refreshing, and what building while employed actually costs you.

What are you building right now?

slides.main_text: I built a SaaS and have **zero paying customers.**
slides.sub_text: Here is what week 1 actually looked like.
After 7 days live. Fully launched. Real people able to sign up.
slides.bubble: I kept refreshing Stripe expecting something to happen. Nothing happened.

slides.main_text: I built it in **evenings and weekends.**
slides.sub_text: While working full-time at a US startup in Malaysia.
slides.sub_text_2: No co-founder. No investors. No announcement.
slides.bubble: My colleagues think I go to bed early.

slides.main_text: I launched **quietly.**
slides.sub_text: No Product Hunt. No X post. No fanfare.
slides.sub_text_2: Just pushed it live and waited to see if anyone noticed.
slides.bubble: The scariest part was not launching. It was nobody noticing.

slides.main_text: The gap between **it is ready** and anyone caring.
slides.sub_text: Nobody is waiting for your product. You have to go find them.
slides.bubble: Week 1 taught me: building is the easy part. Distribution is the job.

slides.main_text: Zero customers does not mean **zero progress.**
slides.sub_text: It means you are still early enough to get it right.
slides.follow_cta: Follow to know what happens next or save this for later.
slides.bubble: Month 2 starts tomorrow. Let's see if the emails start converting.

angle: What working remotely for an overseas startup is actually like
caption: Everyone romanticises working remotely for an overseas startup. Here is what it actually looks like.
threads_text: Working remotely for an overseas startup sounds cool until you are on a late-night Zoom while your friends are out. The pay is decent. The timezone is not. You are always half a day behind everyone who matters. Still beats a lot of alternatives though.

slides.main_text: Working remotely for an **overseas startup** sounds like the dream.
slides.sub_text: Here is what nobody tells you about it.

slides.title: The time tax
slides.sub_text: Hours between me and the rest of the team, every single day.
slides.bubble: My most productive hours are when my boss is asleep. Make of that what you will.

slides.title: The schedule
slides.main_text: Your **mornings are free.** Your evenings are not.
slides.sub_text: All the real meetings happen at the end of your day, so you learn to protect your mornings like they are the only hours you actually own.
slides.bubble: I have perfected the art of looking alert on camera late at night.

slides.title: The math
slides.main_text: The **pay gap** is real and it works in your favour.
slides.sub_text: Headquarters salary, local cost of living. The math is genuinely good, and this is the one part that lives up to the hype.
slides.bubble: Not going to pretend this is not a big part of why most people do it.

slides.title: The downside
slides.main_text: But you are always **half a day behind** everyone who matters.
slides.sub_text: Decisions get made while you sleep, and you catch up in the morning to find the landscape has already shifted.
slides.bubble: Nothing like waking up to 47 Slack messages and a decision that affects your whole week.

slides.title: The opportunity
slides.main_text: So I started building in the **pockets of time I actually own.**
slides.sub_text: 8,471 cold emails out to potential customers. 100 a day, automated. Zero paying customers so far. But the mornings are mine.
slides.bubble: Running a side project while the day job sleeps. Very normal behaviour.

slides.title: The takeaway
slides.main_text: The **timezone gap** is the tax. The free mornings are the opportunity.
slides.sub_text: Still figuring out if I am playing it right.
slides.bubble: Ask me again once the side project actually makes money.

angle: Day 1 — Navigating career: left MBB after 5 years
caption:
I left a $60k/month job at MBB after 5 years. Probably one of the dumbest decisions I have ever made. Here is everything I did not expect.

Have you ever left a job that looked great from the outside? Drop it in the comments.
threads_text: I left a $60k/month job at MBB. Nobody told me what comes next.

slides.main_text: I left a **$60k/month job** at MBB after 5 years.
slides.sub_text: Probably one of the dumbest decisions I have ever made in my life.

slides.title: The Exit
slides.sub_text: Everyone asked why I was leaving. Two years more and I'll get promoted to leadership. It's a stupid decision.
slides.stat_label: years at MBB before I walked away
slides.bubble: Everyone asked why I was leaving but I kept asking myself why I was staying.

slides.title: The Identity
slides.main_text: I used to be **very proud** of my business cards.
slides.sub_text: Now they make me question my identity.
slides.bubble: I still have my old business cards. I keep them as a reminder, not a trophy.

slides.title: The Reality
slides.main_text: The meetings you **complained about** was also routine comfort.
slides.sub_text: The structure, the meetings, the drill, everything was so organized.
slides.bubble: Week one. No meetings, no deliverables, no one to report to. I did not know what to do with myself.

slides.title: The Loss
slides.main_text: Here is what nobody **puts on the list.**
slides.sub_text: The title. The peers. The salary. And knowing exactly what you were supposed to do every day.
slides.bubble: I feel disconnected. I feel like I lost a part of my identity. I don't know who I am.

slides.title: The Takeaway
slides.main_text: Leaving is not the **brave part.** Figuring out who you are after is.
slides.sub_text: Still working on it. I want to share to the world as it happens.

angle: Things I genuinely miss about consulting
caption:
Things I genuinely miss about consulting. Shorter list than I expected. More honest than most people admit.

What do you miss most about a job you left? Comment below.
threads_text: Things I genuinely miss about consulting. Shorter list than I expected.

slides.main_text: Things I genuinely miss about **consulting.**
slides.sub_text: Shorter list than I expected. More honest than most people admit.

slides.title: The Count
slides.sub_text: Things I actually miss. Not things I am supposed to say I miss.
slides.stat_label: things on the honest list. One of them is embarrassing.
slides.bubble: I miss the salary. Including that. Not going to pretend otherwise.

slides.title: The Variety
slides.main_text: A new **problem every 3 months.** That part, I miss.
slides.sub_text: Different industry every few months. No way to stay slow.
slides.bubble: I complained about the pace then. I would not now.

slides.title: The People
slides.main_text: The colleagues who **made you sharper.**
slides.sub_text: Being the least sharp person in the room every day does something to you. I did not expect to miss that pressure.
slides.bubble: I have not had a peer group like that since. That surprised me.

slides.title: The Salary
slides.main_text: I miss the **pay. A lot.** That is the embarrassing one.
slides.sub_text: Not just the pay but the lifestyle, meal budget, business travel, and 5 star hotels.
slides.bubble: I knew the number before I left. I still underestimated how much I would feel it.

slides.title: The Takeaway
slides.main_text: Missing something does not mean you were **wrong to leave.**
slides.sub_text: It means it was real. And real things leave a mark.

angle: I left the best job title I will probably ever have. Here is what nobody tells you comes next.
caption:
I left the best job title I will probably ever have.

Most people warned me about the pay cut. Nobody warned me about the identity part.

What happens when your entire personality was "consultant at [firm]" and then suddenly... it isn't?

This is what that actually felt like.

Have you ever left a prestigious job for something uncertain? What pushed you? Comment below.
threads_text:
I left the best job title I will probably ever have.

Everyone warned me about the pay cut. Nobody warned me that half my personality was just... the firm name.

Now I'm building kakisewa.com. Zero paying customers. Nobody's heard of it.

Honestly the hardest part isn't the uncertainty. It's that nobody asks "where do you work?" the same way anymore.

Have you ever left a prestigious job for something unknown? What pushed you?

slides.main_text: I left the best **job title** I will probably ever have.
slides.sub_text: The identity crisis nobody warns you about when you leave a prestigious job for something unknown.
Years I spent in consulting before I walked away from it
slides.bubble: Long enough for it to become who I was. Short enough that people still ask why I left.

slides.main_text: The **prestige was real.** So was the trap.
slides.sub_text: The brand opened doors. It also became a crutch.
slides.sub_text_2: At some point I stopped knowing if I was good or just well-labeled.
slides.bubble: The firm name was doing a lot of heavy lifting in conversations I didn't notice.

slides.main_text: Nobody outside consulting **cares what tier your firm was.**
slides.sub_text: Inside the bubble it's everything. Outside it's a blank stare.
slides.bubble: First time I said the firm name to a non-corporate person and got nothing back. Humbling.

slides.main_text: Going from **"consultant at X"** to "I'm building something you haven't heard of" is a full identity reset.
slides.sub_text: The old answer to "what do you do" was a conversation starter.
slides.sub_text_2: The new answer is a conversation ender.
slides.bubble: Now I say kakisewa.com and watch people nod politely. Zero paying customers doesn't help the pitch.

slides.main_text: What I thought life after consulting would look like: **freedom, clarity, momentum.**
slides.sub_text: What it actually is: a full-time job at Tarro, a side project with no customers yet, and a lot of quiet.
slides.sub_text_2: The clarity comes eventually. It just doesn't arrive with the resignation letter.
slides.bubble: I genuinely thought leaving would feel like a movie moment. It felt more like a Sunday with no plans.

slides.main_text: The title was never the thing. But you only learn that **after you give it up.**
slides.sub_text: I still have my old business cards somewhere. I keep them as a reminder, not a trophy.
slides.follow_cta: Have you ever left a prestigious job for something uncertain? What pushed you? Comment below.
slides.bubble: Building kakisewa with zero customers is humbling in ways no consulting project ever was. That's probably the point.

angle: First tool I have ever built, started because of my cousins pain point, not to make money
caption:
Still zero customers. But I did not start this to make money.

My cousin would not stop complaining about the same problem every time I saw him. I had never built a tool before in my life. I just wanted to see if I could.

The more I use it, the more holes I find. It is never going to be perfect. I am still glad I started.

Has someone else's pain point ever pushed you to build something? Tell me below.
threads_text:
Building in public: this is the first tool I have ever built. Not for the money, my cousin would not stop complaining about the same problem every time I saw him.

The more I use it, the more holes I find. It will never be perfect. Still glad I started.

slides.main_text: I am building a tool for the most **unsexy market** I could find.
slides.sub_text: Not because it makes money. Because my cousin would not stop complaining about the same problem.

slides.title: The starting point
slides.sub_text: No market research. No spreadsheet of competitors. Just one conversation that stuck with me.
slides.stat_label: tools I had built before this one. This is my first.
slides.bubble: This is tool number one. I am learning as I go.

slides.title: The real reason
slides.main_text: My cousin's **day to day pain point** is the whole reason this exists.
slides.sub_text: He was not pitching me an idea. He was just venting about the same thing, over and over, every time I saw him.
slides.bubble: He was not asking me to build anything. I could not stop thinking about it anyway.

slides.title: Why I am actually doing this
slides.main_text: **Not for the money.** I just wanted to try building something real.
slides.sub_text: I wanted to see if I could take an idea all the way to something someone could actually use, and learn everything in between.
slides.bubble: If this never makes a cent, I will still have learned how to build.

slides.title: The holes
slides.main_text: The more I use it myself, **the more holes I find.**
slides.sub_text: I was sure it was done. Then I opened it again and found three things wrong before breakfast.
slides.bubble: I keep finding rough edges I swore were not there yesterday.

slides.title: The truth
slides.main_text: It will **never be perfect.**
slides.sub_text: I used to think I would ship once it was finished. Turns out finished was never going to happen.
slides.bubble: Perfect was always just an excuse to keep it to myself a little longer.

slides.title: The takeaway
slides.main_text: **I am just glad I started.**
slides.sub_text: Zero paying customers, a dozen rough edges, and I would still do it again.
slides.bubble: Ask me again in six months. For now, I started. That is the whole win.

angle: MBB taught me one skill that transfers to startups. Everything else I had to unlearn.
caption:
MBB taught me one skill that actually transfers to startups. The rest I had to unlearn, and some of it took embarrassingly long.

Swipe to see what stuck and what didn't.

What's the one skill from your current job that would help in a startup? Comment below.
threads_text:
One skill from consulting transfers to startups. Everything else I had to unlearn.

The dangerous one: mistaking analysis for action. I was very good at being thorough. Startups don't reward thorough. They reward moving.

What's the one skill from your job that would actually help in a startup?

slides.main_text: MBB taught me **one skill** that transfers to startups.
slides.sub_text: Everything else I had to unlearn, and some of it took longer than I expected.
Number of skills from 3 years of consulting I use every single day building kakisewa
slides.stat_label: just one skill made the cut
slides.bubble: I thought it would be more. It is not more.

slides.main_text: The one thing: **structure the problem before jumping to solutions**
slides.sub_text: Founders waste weeks building the wrong thing because they never defined what problem they were actually solving.
slides.sub_text_2: This habit from consulting genuinely saves me from myself.
slides.bubble: It also means I spend 10 minutes writing the problem statement before I open my laptop. Worth it.

slides.main_text: What does **not** transfer: slides, frameworks, consensus
slides.sub_text: Nobody is waiting for your 12-slide deck. There is no steering committee. There is no billable hour.
slides.sub_text_2: Consensus-building is how you die slowly in a startup.
slides.bubble: I made a beautiful framework for my kakisewa email strategy. A founder friend said: just send the emails. He was right.

slides.main_text: The dangerous habit: **mistaking analysis for action**
slides.sub_text: Consulting rewards thoroughness. You get paid to be right, not fast.
slides.sub_text_2: Startups reward people who move. Being thorough while the runway burns is just expensive thinking.
slides.bubble: I have 8,471 agents scraped and zero paying customers. Analysis did not cause that. But it did not help either.

slides.main_text: **One skill transfers. The rest is dead weight.**
slides.sub_text: Figure out which habits your current job is rewarding that a startup would punish.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Still working on this myself. kakisewa is live and free right now. Sending 100 cold emails a day and hoping structure beats inertia.

angle: Consulting trained me to be excellent at things that do not matter. Startups cured me of this. Slowly.
caption:
MBB trained me to make things look good. Startups trained me to make things work. These are not the same skill.

I spent years getting very, very good at the wrong things. The transition was humbling.

What is something you optimised perfectly that turned out not to matter? Comment below.
threads_text:
Consulting trained me to be excellent at things that do not matter.

I made pixel-perfect decks. I nailed the narrative arc. I colour-coded the appendix.

The client decided in the first 10 minutes of the meeting.

Startups have a brutal way of showing you the difference between effort and impact. Still learning it honestly.

What did you over-engineer that turned out not to matter?

slides.main_text: Consulting trained me to be **excellent at things that do not matter.**
slides.sub_text: Startups cured me of this. Slower than I expected.
Hours I spent on slide decks that changed nothing at the client
slides.stat_label: hours on slides that moved nothing
slides.bubble: Conservative estimate. I am being kind to myself.

slides.main_text: The client **decided in 10 minutes.** The deck took 3 weeks.
slides.sub_text: Pixel-perfect transitions. Consistent font hierarchy. A appendix nobody opened.
slides.sub_text_2: The decision was made before we walked in the room.
slides.bubble: I once spent two days aligning boxes on a slide. Two days.

slides.main_text: The **consultant trap:** effort feels like impact because it looks like impact.
slides.sub_text: Hours in, polished output, rigorous process. But output is not outcome.
slides.sub_text_2: Consulting rewards the performance of work. Startups punish it.
slides.bubble: Nobody claps for your framework if the thing does not ship.

slides.main_text: A **working feature** beats a perfect plan. Every single time.
slides.sub_text: kakisewa.com is live. Zero paying customers. Two working features.
slides.sub_text_2: That is more real than any deck I ever made.
slides.bubble: My first pitch deck had 14 slides. The product had 2 working features. I had the order wrong.

slides.main_text: The moment I realised I was **optimising for the wrong thing.**
slides.sub_text: I caught myself rewriting copy on a landing page nobody had visited yet.
slides.sub_text_2: Classic consultant move. Polish the thing. Avoid the scary thing.
slides.bubble: The scary thing was sending the cold email. The copy was fine.

slides.main_text: **Excellent execution** on the wrong thing is still the wrong thing.
slides.sub_text: The skill is not doing things well. It is picking the right things to do at all.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: What is something you optimised perfectly that turned out not to matter? Comment below.

angle: I took a significant pay cut to join a startup. Here are the honest numbers.
caption:
I left a job that pays RM55k/month to take a 40% pay cut and join a startup.

My fiancee never signed up for it. She started covering lunches, doing the groceries, swapping date nights out for staying in. Never once complained.

The upside is not in ringgit yet. It is the people around me now, and how much I am learning from them.

Is it worth it? I do not know. Do I regret it? At least not yet.

What is the biggest risk you have taken for work you actually wanted to do? Tell me below.
threads_text:
I left a job that pays RM55k/month. Took a 40% cut to join a startup.

The hours did not get lighter. The guilt (watching my fiancee quietly start covering lunches and groceries) got heavier.

But I am learning more from the founders around me than I ever did from consulting frameworks.

Is it worth it? I do not know. Do I regret it? At least not yet.

slides.main_text: I left a job that pays **RM55k/month**.
slides.sub_text: The honest numbers, and whether it was worth it.
My MBB base salary in Malaysia versus what I make now at Tarro
slides.stat_label: less than I made at MBB
slides.bubble: I told my parents the new number. They went quiet. Then they asked if the company had medical benefits.

slides.main_text: The **lifestyle change** hit me first.
slides.sub_text: Fewer dinners out. A longer pause before every non-essential purchase. Not dramatic on any single day. Just constant.
slides.bubble: I have started noticing when a restaurant has a cheaper lunch set. That is new behaviour for me.

slides.main_text: Then it hit **someone else too.**
slides.sub_text: My fiancee did not sign up for a pay cut. I did. She started paying for lunches, doing the groceries, swapping date nights out for staying in.
slides.bubble: She has never once complained about it. That somehow makes the guilt worse, not better.

slides.main_text: The **hours** did not get lighter.
slides.sub_text: Tarro by day, kakisewa by night. Same late finishes as MBB, just a different reason for them. I did not trade hard work for less hard work. I traded it for different work.
slides.bubble: Nobody tells you 'follow what you actually want' still means working past midnight. It just feels different at midnight.

slides.main_text: So why do it? **The people.**
slides.sub_text: I am surrounded by founders and operators who think completely differently from consultants. I learn something new almost every week just from being near them.
slides.bubble: At MBB I learned frameworks. Here I am learning how people actually build things from zero.

slides.main_text: It is **not paying off** yet. Not in ringgit.
slides.sub_text: Kakisewa has zero paying customers and a cold email list of 30,000 agents. The ROI is not mathing yet. I am still showing up anyway.
slides.bubble: 30,000 cold emails and zero replies that convert into paying customers. Humbling in a very specific way.

slides.main_text: **Is it worth it?** I do not know.
slides.sub_text: Do I regret it? At least not yet.

angle: Consulting actively trains you to be a terrible founder. I am proving this in real time.
caption:
Consulting gives you a great salary and a terrible founder brain.

I have three reasons why. I have lived all three.

The scariest part is I did not notice any of them until I was already building kakisewa and making all the mistakes in real time.

What is the biggest habit your current job has given you that would hurt you as a founder? Comment below.
threads_text:
Consulting trains you to be a terrible founder. Here is the proof.

You get wired to reduce risk, not take it. You let clients define the problem. You optimise for looking right over being right.

I am building kakisewa.com with zero paying customers and all three of these showing up constantly.

What does your current job train you to do that would wreck you as a founder?

slides.main_text: Consulting actively trains you to be **a terrible founder.**
slides.sub_text: Three reasons why, and I have lived all of them.
Founder habits that consulting actively trains out of you
slides.bubble: I spent a week researching whether to use Notion or Obsidian for notes. A real founder would have picked one in 5 minutes.

slides.main_text: Reason 1: You are **wired to reduce risk,** not take it.
slides.sub_text: Consulting rewards you for spotting what can go wrong and building a slide about it.
slides.sub_text_2: Founding rewards you for shipping anyway. These are opposite instincts.
slides.bubble: I have a 3-tab spreadsheet analysing cold email risk. I should have just sent the emails two weeks earlier.

slides.main_text: Reason 2: You are used to **clients defining the problem.**
slides.sub_text: In consulting, someone hands you the question. Your job is to answer it well.
slides.sub_text_2: As a founder, choosing the right problem to solve is the whole job. Nobody hands you that.
slides.bubble: I kept waiting for my users to tell me exactly what to build. They do not know either. That was a surprise.

slides.main_text: Reason 3: Optimised for **looking right,** not being right.
slides.sub_text: MBB trains you to never walk into a room without the answer already locked.
slides.sub_text_2: Founding means being publicly wrong, repeatedly, and updating fast. The deck is never done.
slides.bubble: kakisewa.com is live. Zero paying customers. This post exists. Make of that what you will.

slides.main_text: The caveat: **some of us recover.**
slides.sub_text: I am trying. Slowly. With 8,471 scraped agents and 100 cold emails going out per day as proof I eventually moved.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: What is the biggest habit your current job has given you that would hurt you as a founder? Comment below.

angle: I spent 3 years making slides for clients. Not one of them shipped anything people could use.
caption:
3 years of slides. Zero of them shipped anything real.

Consulting teaches you to think clearly and communicate well. It does not teach you to build. Those are different skills and I had to learn the second set from scratch.

The first time I pushed something to production, nobody saw it. I was still happier than any client presentation I ever gave.

What have you shipped that you are proud of — even if nobody saw it? Comment below.
threads_text:
I spent 3 years making slides. Not one client shipped anything I could actually use.

Now I'm building kakisewa.com at midnight after my day job. It's live. Zero paying customers.

The first time I pushed to production, nobody saw it. Still felt better than any deck I ever presented.

What's the last thing you actually shipped?

slides.main_text: I spent 3 years making **slides** for clients. Not one of them shipped anything people could use.
slides.sub_text: The skills do not transfer the way you think.
Years in consulting before I shipped anything people could actually use
slides.bubble: And I was considered good at my job.

slides.main_text: Making slides teaches you to **communicate**. It does not teach you to build.
slides.sub_text: Two different muscles. Consulting trains one of them very well.
slides.sub_text_2: The other one you learn by doing embarrassing first versions.
slides.bubble: My first version of kakisewa looked like a school project.

slides.main_text: The **product** reveals whether your thinking was right. The slide never does.
slides.sub_text: A client can nod at a deck. Real users just leave.
slides.bubble: Zero paying customers so far. The product is being very honest with me.

slides.main_text: What I had to learn from scratch: **shipping ugly things** and being okay with it.
slides.sub_text: In consulting, you polish until the slide looks confident.
slides.sub_text_2: In building, you ship before it feels ready. Every time.
slides.bubble: kakisewa is live. It is not pretty. It is there.

slides.main_text: A **shipped product** hits different from a delivered deck.
slides.sub_text: The first time I pushed to production, nobody saw it.
slides.sub_text_2: I was still happier than any client presentation I ever gave.
slides.bubble: That was the moment I understood what I had been missing.

slides.main_text: Consulting made me sharper. Building made me **honest**.
slides.sub_text: The slide tells you what you want to hear. The product does not.
slides.follow_cta: Follow to know what happens next or save this for later.
slides.bubble: What have you shipped that you are proud of — even if nobody saw it? Comment below.

angle: A startup taught me something consulting never did. It was not what I expected.
caption:
A startup taught me something consulting never did.

I spent years in MBB learning how to move fast. Tight deadlines, late nights, decks submitted at 11:59pm.

Turns out that was not fast.

At a startup, fast means something completely different. And the first time I saw it up close, I felt like I had been lied to my whole career.

What is the biggest speed difference you have noticed between big company and small company? Comment below.
threads_text:
A startup taught me something consulting never did. It was not what I expected.

In consulting, moving fast meant finishing the deck before the client walked in.

At a startup, moving fast means shipping before someone else does.

Very different stakes. Very different clock.

Big company vs small company — what is the biggest speed gap you have noticed?

slides.main_text: A startup taught me something **consulting never did.**
slides.sub_text: Speed. Real speed.
Hours from idea to shipped at a startup vs weeks in consulting
slides.bubble: I once spent 3 weeks on a deliverable a startup would have shipped, iterated, and shipped again.

slides.main_text: In consulting, **moving fast** means finishing the deck before the client meeting.
slides.sub_text: The urgency is real. The output is a PowerPoint.
slides.sub_text_2: Nobody is shipping anything.
slides.bubble: Slides do not go live. Code does.

slides.main_text: At a startup, **moving fast** means shipping before someone else does.
slides.sub_text: The competitor is not in the room. They are already building.
slides.bubble: The clock is always running. You just cannot see it.

slides.main_text: First time I saw a feature go from **Slack message to live** in two days.
slides.sub_text: I genuinely did not know that was possible.
slides.bubble: I asked if that was normal. Everyone looked at me like I was slow.

slides.main_text: **Speed is a competitive advantage.** Slowness is a silent killer.
slides.sub_text: You do not feel it until someone else is already three versions ahead of you.
slides.bubble: kakisewa is live. Zero paying customers. But it is live. That part matters.

slides.main_text: The best thing consulting taught me was **structure.** The best thing a startup taught me was to ship the thing.
slides.sub_text: Both matter. But only one of them compounds.
slides.follow_cta: Follow to know what happens next or save this for later.
slides.bubble: What is the biggest speed difference you have noticed between big company and small company? Comment below.

angle: I shipped my first product and felt like a fraud the whole time. Nobody warned me about this part.
caption:
I shipped kakisewa and told exactly zero people about it for two weeks.

Not because I was doing a quiet launch strategy. Because I was terrified.

Imposter syndrome when you're a founder hits different from when you're a consultant. In consulting, someone above you signs off. Someone validates the work. The deck gets approved. You're protected by the process.

When it's your product, there's no one to approve it. It's just you and whatever you built and the very real possibility that it reflects exactly how you think — and your thinking is wrong.

I pushed through it the only way I knew how. Shipped anyway. Waited for the market to tell me I was an idiot.

Still waiting, honestly. But at least the thing exists now.

Have you ever felt like a fraud doing something you were actually capable of? Comment below.
threads_text:
I shipped my first product and felt like a fraud the whole time.

Not imposter syndrome in the vague motivational poster sense. The specific kind where you've built something that is a direct reflection of how you think, and you're not sure your thinking is good.

Told zero people for two weeks after launch.

Have you ever felt like a fraud doing something you were actually capable of?

slides.main_text: I shipped my first product and felt like a **fraud** the whole time.
slides.sub_text: Nobody warned me this part was coming.
Number of people I told about the product for the first two weeks after launch
slides.bubble: Not a strategy. Just fear with a good excuse.

slides.main_text: Imposter syndrome hits **differently** when you're the founder.
slides.sub_text: In consulting, someone above you validated the work. The deck got approved. You were covered.
slides.sub_text_2: Here, no one signs off. It's just you.
slides.bubble: I genuinely missed having a senior partner to blame.

slides.main_text: The product is a **direct reflection of your thinking.**
slides.sub_text: That's what makes it terrifying. If the product is wrong, you're wrong.
slides.bubble: Years of MBB training and I still wasn't ready for this specific feeling.

slides.main_text: So I did the only thing that made sense: **shipped anyway.**
slides.sub_text: Decided I'd wait for the market to tell me I was an idiot instead of telling myself first.
slides.bubble: Market hasn't said much yet. Zero paying customers. The silence is its own kind of feedback.

slides.main_text: The fraud feeling doesn't mean you're **wrong. It means you care.**
slides.sub_text: The people who say they never feel it are either lying or not paying close enough attention.
slides.follow_cta: Follow to know what happens next or save this for later.
slides.bubble: I still feel it sometimes. Shipped 8,471 cold emails anyway.

angle: The alumni network sounds powerful. Here is what it is actually useful for when you are building.
caption:
Everyone says the alumni network is your biggest asset when you start something.

Here is what mine has actually been useful for — and where it completely fell flat.

Honest answer: 2 meaningful intros from my entire consulting network. That is it.

The network is real. The problems I am solving just do not match who is in it.

What network has actually been useful for your work — alumni, industry, or online? Comment below.
threads_text:
The alumni network sounds powerful. Here is what it is actually useful for when you are building.

I asked a former McKinsey colleague for cold email strategy advice. He had never sent a cold email in his life.

Credibility? Yes. Warm intros to property agents in Malaysia? Absolutely not.

What network has actually moved the needle for you — alumni, industry, or something else?

slides.main_text: The alumni network sounds powerful. Here is what it is **actually useful for** when you are building.
slides.sub_text: Honest answer: yes and no, and the split is not where you expect it.
Meaningful introductions from my entire consulting network that actually moved things forward
slides.stat_label: out of years of MBB connections
slides.bubble: I am not bitter. I am just counting.

slides.main_text: It does help with **credibility signals** and warm intros to people who will take your call
slides.sub_text: If you need someone to reply to a cold LinkedIn message, the MBB brand still works.
slides.sub_text_2: Getting a first meeting is easier. That part is real.
slides.bubble: Useful for doors. Less useful for what happens after you walk through them.

slides.main_text: The problem: **nobody in my consulting network is a property agent**
slides.sub_text: kakisewa.com is built for Malaysian rental agents. My network is bankers, consultants, and ex-consultants.
slides.sub_text_2: That is why I scraped 8,471 agents and set up cold email. The warm intro path does not exist here.
slides.bubble: I asked a former McKinsey colleague for cold email strategy advice. He had never sent a cold email in his life.

slides.main_text: The alumni network is **built for corporate problems**, not startup problems
slides.sub_text: Corporate problems: navigating org politics, landing a role, getting a reference.
slides.sub_text_2: Startup problems: finding your first 10 customers, pricing a product nobody has heard of.
slides.bubble: Different game. The playbook does not transfer the way everyone implies it does.

slides.main_text: **Build the right network** for what you are actually doing
slides.sub_text: For kakisewa, the useful people are property agents, PropTech founders, and people who have done B2B sales to fragmented markets.
slides.sub_text_2: Most of them are not in any alumni directory I have access to.
slides.bubble: Currently finding more useful people on X and Reddit than anywhere else. Make of that what you will.

slides.main_text: Your alumni network is an **asset with a specific use case** — know what it is and is not
slides.sub_text: Mine is useful for credibility and corporate doors. For selling to property agents in Malaysia, I am on my own.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: What network has actually been useful for your work — alumni, industry, or online? Comment below.

angle: I picked the most unsexy market possible.
caption:
Day 2. Still zero customers. But I picked this market on purpose.

Most people said it was not exciting enough to build for. I thought: good, fewer people will try to compete.

Swipe to see why I chose boring over cool, and whether it is actually smart or just cope.

Unsexy market or cool idea, which would you build? Vote in comments: U or C
threads_text:
Day 2 of building in public. I picked property agents as my market. They still track contracts in WhatsApp. Nobody thinks it is exciting. I think that is the point.

Poll: which would you build?
U = Unsexy market with real pain
C = Cool idea with hype

slides.main_text: I picked the most **unsexy market** possible.
slides.sub_text: Property agents who still track contracts in WhatsApp. And I think that is the point.

slides.title: The market
slides.sub_text: Property agents with no digital contract system, tracking everything in WhatsApp and sticky notes.
slides.stat_label: agents found. 100 cold emails going out every day.
slides.bubble: I have their emails. I do not have their money. Yet.

slides.title: The gap
slides.main_text: **Nobody is building for this market.**
slides.sub_text: Which either means it is a wide open opportunity, or everyone already tried and left. I am choosing to believe the first one.
slides.bubble: This is the part where I find out which.

slides.title: The validation
slides.main_text: The problem is **obvious once you talk to one agent.**
slides.sub_text: Contracts in WhatsApp, renewals tracked in their head, and nobody built a tool because nobody thought it was worth building.
slides.bubble: First agent I talked to just uses notes on their phone. That was my product validation.

slides.title: The advantage
slides.main_text: **Unsexy markets have real paying customers** and fewer competitors.
slides.sub_text: The pain is specific, the buyers exist, and nobody is pitching them at TechCrunch.
slides.bubble: My friends thought I was building something boring. I think boring pays rent.

slides.title: The pitch
slides.main_text: I chose this over a **'cool' idea** because the pain is specific.
slides.sub_text: Specific pain means the customer already knows they have a problem before you explain it, which makes the sales conversation much easier.
slides.bubble: Cool ideas need you to convince people they have a problem. Hard pass.

slides.title: The takeaway
slides.main_text: **Boring problems, real customers,** less noise.
slides.sub_text: Still zero paying customers, but I know exactly who I am building for.
slides.bubble: Unsexy market or cool idea, which would you build? Drop U or C in the comments.

angle: Things I do not miss about consulting. This list is much longer.
caption:
Six things I do not miss about consulting. The list is longer but I had to stop somewhere.

(SOD = start of day, EOD = end of day alignment meetings. Daily check-ins to align on priorities and wrap up. Standard in consulting.)

What is the one thing you would never go back to from a previous job? Comment below.
threads_text:
Things I do not miss about consulting:

- performing busyness for the client
- solving problems that felt urgent and irrelevant a year later
- managing up instead of building anything
- travelling for a meeting that was definitely an email
- the firm name doing more work than I did
- timesheets

This list is longer. What would you never go back to?

slides.main_text:
Things I do not miss about consulting.
**This list is much longer.**
slides.sub_text: Six things, including one that sounds petty but is not.
I counted. Stopped at six because I have a day job.
slides.stat_label: things I do not miss. Honest count.
slides.bubble: The first week I did not have to submit a timesheet, I felt like I had been released from something.

slides.main_text: **Performing busyness** for the client
slides.sub_text: Looking occupied in a room is not the same as doing anything useful.
slides.sub_text_2: We were very good at looking occupied.
slides.bubble: Slide decks at 11pm that nobody asked for.

slides.main_text: Solving problems that felt **urgent in the room**
slides.sub_text: Revisit the recommendations a year later. Half of it did not matter.
slides.sub_text_2: The other half was already decided before we walked in.
slides.bubble: We called it impact. I'm not sure what it was.

slides.main_text: **Managing up** more than building anything
slides.sub_text: A lot of energy went into keeping stakeholders comfortable.
slides.sub_text_2: Very little went into making something that worked.
slides.bubble: I got good at it. That is the part that worries me.

slides.main_text: Travelling for work that was **an email**
slides.sub_text: Fly in Monday. Fly out Thursday. Repeat until your loyalty tier means something.
slides.bubble: The meeting could have been a Slack. The Slack could have been nothing.

slides.main_text: The **firm name** carrying your identity
slides.sub_text: You introduce yourself and people react to the logo, not to you.
slides.sub_text_2: Leaving tells you which one they were actually impressed by.
slides.bubble: Built kakisewa. Zero customers. That one is mine though.

slides.main_text: The petty one: **SOD and EOD alignment.**
slides.sub_text: We called it the manufacturing process. Makes you focus but also creates a false sense of urgency.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: For those who do not know: SOD = Start of Day, EOD = End of Day alignment. Daily check-ins every consultant has sat through.

angle: Everyone says quit and go all in. Here is why I have not. It is not fear.
caption:
Everyone says quit your job and go all in. I have not. And it is not fear — it is math.

I ran the numbers. The job funds the experiment. The experiment might make the job optional one day.

Have you ever gone all in on something? What made you pull the trigger? Comment below.
threads_text: Everyone says go all in and quit your job. I have not, and it is not fear. I have 8,471 agents scraped, 100 cold emails going out daily, zero paying customers, and a full-time job that keeps the lights on while I find out if this works. Going all in is romantic. Going broke is not. Have you ever quit something stable to bet on yourself?

slides.main_text:
Everyone says quit and **go all in.**
I have not.
It is not fear.
slides.sub_text: It is math.
Months of runway if I quit my job tomorrow
slides.bubble: 8 months sounds okay until month 3 when you are still at zero customers.

slides.main_text: **Runway is not a vibe.** It is a number.
slides.sub_text: Savings divided by monthly burn. That is it.
slides.sub_text_2: Mine does not survive long enough to wait out a slow B2B sales cycle.
slides.bubble: I did the spreadsheet. It was not encouraging.

slides.main_text: **Going all in** is romantic. Going broke is not.
slides.sub_text: Every founder who says 'just commit' had savings, a partner's income, or a safety net they are not mentioning.
slides.bubble: I have seen the LinkedIn posts. Nobody mentions the safety net.

slides.main_text: The job **funds the experiment.**
slides.sub_text: The experiment might one day make the job optional.
slides.sub_text_2: Right now kakisewa.com is live, 8,471 agents are in the pipeline, and I have zero paying customers. The job stays.
slides.bubble: This is not settling. This is not running out of money before getting an answer.

slides.main_text: I will quit when I hit a **specific revenue number.** Not a feeling.
slides.sub_text: Not when it feels right. Not when I feel confident enough.
slides.sub_text_2: A milestone. A real one. Written down.
slides.bubble: Feelings are not a financial plan.

slides.main_text: **Going all in** only makes sense when the math says you can survive being wrong.
slides.sub_text: Until then, the job is not the enemy. It is the bet hedge.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Have you ever gone all in on something? What made you pull the trigger? Comment below.

angle: If I could go back, would I take the consulting job again? The honest answer surprised me.
caption:
If I could go back, would I take the consulting job again?

Honest answer: yes. And also I would leave it sooner.

Both things are true.

Swipe for the full breakdown — what MBB actually gave me, and the one thing I wish I had done differently.

Is there a job or decision you would go back and do again — just differently? Comment below.
threads_text:
If I could go back, would I take the consulting job again?

I thought the answer was obvious. It wasn't.

Yes — but I would leave one year earlier. The credential opens doors. The experience builds a pattern library. But staying too long is its own tax.

Would you take your last big career decision again, just differently?

slides.main_text: If I could go back, would I take the **consulting job** again?
slides.sub_text: What I would do the same — and what I would change.
The year I started in consulting.
slides.bubble: Which means I have had about seven years to think about whether it was worth it. Still not fully sure. Ask me again in seven more.

slides.main_text: I would take it again — for what it taught me to **see**, not what it taught me to **do**
slides.sub_text: Frameworks are fine. But the real output is pattern recognition.
slides.sub_text_2: You stop seeing problems as new. You start seeing them as variations of something you have already solved.
slides.bubble: This is the thing nobody tells you before you join. The decks are not the point.

slides.main_text: The **credential** opened doors. The **experience** built the library.
slides.sub_text: MBB on a CV gets you in the room. What you did inside the work is what you actually keep.
slides.sub_text_2: I use that pattern library every time I scope kakisewa — what is the real problem, who owns it, what does a good enough solution look like.
slides.bubble: Genuinely did not expect consulting to be useful when I am cold emailing 8,471 rental agents. And yet.

slides.main_text: What I would change: **leave one year earlier**
slides.sub_text: The last year was mostly repetition with a better title.
slides.sub_text_2: You know when you have learned everything the environment has to offer. Most people stay anyway. I did.
slides.bubble: One year does not sound like much until you are building something at 11pm after a full day at Tarro and you think — okay, that year mattered.

slides.main_text: The question is not "was it worth it" — it is "**what did you do with what you learned**"
slides.sub_text: The job is an input. What you build after is the output.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Is there a job or decision you would go back and do again — just differently? Comment below.
